comments.content:
[IMAGE:0]It's coming up on midnight, so here we go with a new Magic Monday. The picture is of Carl von Reichenbach, a brilliant chemist -- he's the guy who figured out how to extract paraffin out of crude petroleum -- who ended up on the wrong side of the scientific establishment by running a long series of controlled, double-blinded experiments to research the life force and the capacity of human beings to perceive it. As usual, the response of the scientific establishment wasn't to try to replicate his work and see if he was right, but to pour on the ridicule and insist that he was wrong because he was wrong because he was wrong, so there. (There's been a long, long sequence of researchers into the same thing who got the same treatment; I may feature a number of them here.)

Ask me anything about occultism and I'll do my best to answer it. Any question received by midnight Monday Eastern time will get an answer, though I'm going to have somewhat limited email access tomorrow and it may be Tuesday or even Wednesday before I get to them all. If you're in a hurry, or suspect you may be the 143,916th person to ask a question, please check out the very rough version 1.0 of The Magic Monday FAQ here.

I've had several people ask about tipping me for answers here, and though I certainly don't require that I won't turn it down. You can use the button below to access my online tip jar. 

With that said, have at it! 

***This Magic Monday is now closed -- and yes,this means you. See you next week!***

comments.title: Dream Taxonomy and Topography
comments.content:
I've written a short essay on some thoughts I've been developing on the landscape dreams here: https://violetcabra.dreamwidth.org/1896.html

JMG and others, especially the oneironauts among us, I'm curious what you all think of this schema, what is conceptually helpful and what isn't?

replies.title: Re: Dream Taxonomy and Topography
replies.content:
My dreams don't really extend over most of the territory you've mapped. On the left-right axis (thoughts to emotions), mine never go over to the thought side and only touch on emotion now and again, and when they do it's not a flow of emotion but simply a single emotional state that colors the dream. They do always have a sequence -- I've never had a timeless dream. The nearest equivalent of your "up and down" dimension is marked by light intensity -- I used to have nightmares of being in pitch darkness and fighting a shadowy being that was unaffected by anything I did. (Those stopped when I realized that other things on that plane could harm him, and I started using nasty little martial arts tricks to slam him repeatedly cranium first into hard objects or the like. I've worried now and then that if I ever get into a really serious fight I'll do the same thing out of reflex and end up with a corpse on my hands.) As for transparency-opacity and meaning-meaninglessness, my dreams are pretty consistently a jumble of incoherent images, reminiscent of nothing so much as the Lord Chancellor's nightmare song from Gilbert and Sullivan's Iolanthe. Even when I wake up with one of those spoken dream-messages in my mind, it's not much help: for example, knowing with absolute certainty that Faraz is actually Pharez, and is real. (That's an actual example.)

That said, I gather from dream literature I've read that my dreams are pretty atypical, so others may find your taxonomy more useful.

replies.title: Re: Dream Taxonomy and Topography
replies.content:
Many thanks for engaging with the essay so thoroughly! All of that makes sense. Interestingly, the "up and down" axis is often marked by light intensity in my dreams -- but not always!

I wonder if there are ways that humans *can* use language to describe the nonsensical and opaque aspects of dreams, or if this is an issue of the very limitations of language. For this reason, I hope other people compare their dream research with mine -- as you have so generously done -- to see how where my analysis is on the "personal to universal" axis ;)

replies.title: Re: Dream Taxonomy and Topography
replies.content:
I have a large variety of dreams, but one recurring location I've visited for many years is a steampunk-dieselpunk version of my home town and the surrounding area. Railways everywhere, coking plants, gasworks, power stations with massive cooling towers - it's awesome.

But when the financial crash happened in 2008, the effect spread into the dream world. Formerly booming heavy industries were brought to ruin. A previously bustling workshop with heavy machine tools, left abandoned. A huge industrial plant a couple of miles long had about eight parallel rail lines running along next to it, always busy with freight trains. The next time I saw it, half was derelict and the rest reduced to rubble. As I was looking at it, a Class 47 locomotive rolled past, pulling a pathetically short string of three goods wagons.

I haven't remembered a dream from there in quite a while. I want to go back and see more of it. Even in desolation it's still beautiful.

replies.title: Re: Dream Taxonomy and Topography
replies.content: I think worth delving into further detail are instances of recurring dreams (I have the same dream on two separate nights, potentially years apart from another) and serial dreams (I have a dream that is clearly intended to be a "sequel" of some prior dream, on a separate night.)  My dream experience seems to make frequent use of these and I suspect others do as well (especially for recurring dreams.)

replies.title: Re: Dream Taxonomy and Topography
replies.content: Good point. To my mind, recurring and serial dreams clearly are on the "forward and backwards" axis of time within my schema.

replies.title: Re: Dream Taxonomy and Topography
replies.content:
Hello Violet,

Thanks for this. I find your axes to be very true to my experience, but I don't experience the full range. Definitely the thought<--> emotion axis, and the opaque<-->transparent, and the meaning continuum is also present. But very rarely the up <--> down.

There is one more axis that I occasionally experience though: the feeling that a person I encounter is more or less real. A lot of the time the people I encounter in dreams feel like...dreams. But occasionally I meet someone or something that feels much more real. Sometimes the feeling is so strong that I wake up because I don't want them to see me and know that I, too, am real. I am still not sure what to make of these.

-Ms. Krieger

replies.title: Re: Dream Taxonomy and Topography
replies.content:
Hi, Violet. I really liked what you said about dreams being the real experience of the astral self. It's really close to things I've said about dreams. I consider the dreamworld to be a real place, where I have real experiences. The reason I take this tack is because anything else feels reductive and like I'm distorting the dream to fit some intellectual notion.

For me, though, everything in a dream has meaning. The question isn't whether or not it has meaning, but whether or not I choose at this time to pay attention to the meaning of that particular image. Some other part of the dream may feel more immediate and pressing, and so I might give that part more attention, but I would never say something in my dream was meaningless. Keeping in mind, of course, that dream meanings are frequently not at all obvious to the waking mind. :)

I've never done much lucid dreaming because I like to let the astral self or the dreamworld tell me what it wants me to know, instead of trying to impose the values of my waking self onto it.

I think it's great that you're writing about this stuff. Some of the most significant experiences in my life have come through dreams.

Tori Minard

comments.title: Occultism and a few other things...
comments.content:
Hi John
1. So… I took a bit of inspiration from WoH and bought a Gypsy Witch playing card deck. The cards are very friendly, but instructions for them do not abound. At this point, I’m reading them right side up because nothing I’ve encountered suggests changed meanings for upside down or sideways. (Or connected the playing cards with the pictures and numbers.) Are there any methods, titles, or websites you recommend?

2. I noticed the Adamic language (or was it something else?) in Red Hook. Similarly, the Adamic script has a significant role in Lovecraft Country – changing letters to move rituals forward and so forth. Is the language worth the time of aspiring occultists, or is it a failed theory that didn’t survive the scrutiny of modern linguistics? (Or something in between?)

3. While I realize you aren’t reading much weird to avoid influencing your own writing right now, might you say a few words about the problem of pastiche – well, the bad kind – and how to avoid writing it? Panshin & Panshin are particularly harsh critics of me-too writers in The World Beyond the Hill: Science Fiction and the Quest for Transcendence, hence my wish for creating genuine weird through the WoH RPG. (“Ooze” by Anthony Rud comes to mind – his other successes notwithstanding.)

4. Would you be so kind as to give a bit more discussion of how you developed the Church of Starry Wisdom in your WoH series and RPG? You’d mentioned the utopian streak and its influence, and I’d love to hear more. Also, the secret calendar intrigues me… new thought or… ?

5. And from the department of wait-just-a-doggone-minute-here: I stumbled across an RPG titled Ribbon Drive that lets you and your pals role play a road trip without going farther than the kitchen for more soda pop. Is it just me, or is there something very troubling lurking behind a fun evening in and a Kunstlerian chuckle? (I might try it anyway.)

6. One last thing – I’ve listened to the composers you’ve mentioned in previous Magic Mondays. Despite knowing just enough about music theory to realize there is lots of meat on the bones, I don’t know what the music is supposed to do for or to me. Advice?

Many thanks for the opportunity to talk with you and your friends in this venue.
Rusty

replies.title: Re: Occultism and a few other things...
replies.content:
An occultist with linguistic training here:

2) Both. As linguistics it's horrible, as something for Christian/Jewish occultists, it's apparently worth looking into.

replies.title: Re: Occultism and a few other things...
replies.content:
1) I don't use reversals with the Gypsy Witch deck. I didn't find much of use online or in print -- there are some good books on the Lenormand deck, of which the Gypsy Witch deck is an expansion, but that only helps you for a little over half the cards. So I basically worked out a system on first principles, with the help of my characters. If I ever get a contract to do a WoH divination deck, I'll include that in the book that comes with it.

2) No, the material in Red Hook isn't the Adamic language or an imitation thereof -- in the fictive universe of the novels, remember, the first language that human beings happened to speak is no big deal, since there were plenty of other intelligent species here before we got here and will be plenty of others after us. I modeled the words of power on those in the Sixth and Seventh Books of Solomon, without using any actual words of power -- that's a common and useful trick for writing faux-magical incantations, and in this case furthered my goal of writing fantasy rooted in American culture. As an occultist, I don't find the Adamic-language conceit useful, but your mileage may vary.

3) I don't know that there's a system for doing it, other than writing a lot and reading a lot of diverse things.

4) Oh, it's a jumble of 19th century American alternative spirituality, 19th century American fraternalism, old-fashioned occultism, and shale I made up out of whole cloth. I could doubtless write fifty thousand words about it, but I'd be making it up as I went along.

5) That sounds really rather dull -- unless you were to do odd things with it. Stil, your mileage may vary.

6) I have no idea how to teach listening to classical music; anyone else?

replies.content:

That is to say, yes, for only 14.95-- quite a deal. Professor Greenberg has several other Great Courses available on www.audible.com, by the way, and I would recommend them all.

replies.title: Re: Occultism and a few other things...
replies.content: Following on from what the other Anon said about The Great Courses on classical music, there are several on Audible (Amazon owned): https://www.audible.com/ep/the-great-courses-arts

replies.title: Re: Occultism and a few other things...
replies.content:
Concerning music you could enroll in some course to understand how harmonies play out and how to read music sheet, and so forth.
But when it comes to knowing what music is supposed to do for or to you there is no way other then listening and waiting...

In my childhood I’d listen when my father put his albums to play. Classical Russian music by Rimsky-Korsakov, Borodin, Tchaikovsky...
One day during breakfast as I was listening to Mussorgsky’s “Pictures at an Exhibition” suddenly a whole story unfolded in my mind – one of medieval times, knights, sword fighting and maidens to be protected. I was so elated that I briefly considered a career in motion picture industry,hehehe.

On another occasion (I was 8 years old) while my parents had gone to the movies I sat in our living room, in the dark, listening to Beethoven’s sonata for violin and piano No. 7
The music grabbed me and I felt as if I had been present at the time Beethoven composed it. I'm 65 now and still have this feeling every time I listen to this particular piece of music.

Of course, in my youth, I would rather listen to The Beatles but the fact is we didn’t have any Beatles albums at home. We had classical music and that is what I listened to. Still it was “my choice” to listen. You have to make an effort to listen.
Let it seep through your ears until it reaches your mind and most importantly your heart. Then it will finaly click.

replies.title: Re: Occultism and a few other things...
replies.content:
Rusty,

“….. I don’t know what the music is supposed to do for or to me. Advice?”

What everyone else is saying about the Great Courses and Professor Greenberg is spot on and good advice. If you want an intro without having to invest the hours to go through his excellent lectures, I would suggest the YouTube channel “Inside the Score” by Oscar Osicki.

He has a whole playlist entitled “How to Listen to Classical Music” and does a good job getting into the nuts and bolts of answering your question. However this series still runs over an hour.

If you want an even quicker introduction, I’d recommend his video “Discovering Classical Music #1 - Holst The Planets.” Here, like a good fiction author, he “shows” rather than “tells” why the music is so powerful and effective.

He discusses Neptune:

“This movement takes you into another space or another world altogether. The most amazing part of it all is when you suddenly realize this celestial sound has entered from apparently nowhere. A choir of upper voices is singing from another room. We hear this sound but we cannot see the source of it. Apparently it’s coming from another planet altogether. Remember, this was before recorded music was commonly available, so the effect on the audiences must hav been totally entrancing or mind-blowing.” ( source, Neptune's at the 18:00 mark)

Entrancing? Yes. Mind-blowing? Oh Yes. That’s what I love about the genre so much. Speaking for myself, Oscar’s words describe perfectly what classical music does for, and to, me.

comments.content:
Last week I mentioned the idea Eris may have functioned as a planet, and I spent the past week looking further into it, and it seems to have a great deal going for it.

If, as seems increasingly likely Eris was an astrological planet, it should be possible to determine which sign it rules. I initially limited myself to the four where it could take over without doing something really weird.

This left four: Taurus, Gemini, Virgo, and Libra. There are three ways I looked into it, all three of which point the same way. The first was looking at where I express Eridian influences; the second was looking at symbolism; and the third was looking at changes to the sign. Martian Scorpio is very different from Plutonic Scorpio, and a similar principle seems likely to apply here.

I've mostly worked with old astrological texts, and so I looked online, and it seems like a few places discuss anger and violence as being Tauran traits, and Taurus as described online seems to be a little different from Taurus in the older texts; my chart express Eridian influences in the houses with Taurus on the cusp; and symbolically Taurus fits Eris better than the other signs.

Are there other ways to check, aside from casting dozens of charts and seeing if this consistently fits? I know that's going to be a next step, but I'd like to avoid doing a large number of charts and then realizing I missed something really basic.

(I also figure given the interest last week, that throwing this out there will probably get a few other people checking too, and maybe people will see things I missed. Thank you all, you gave me a lot of ideas last week!)

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: As far as I know, the only way to do it is to study lots of charts. There may be another way, but I'm still only an amateur astrologer, you know.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: You might want to read books on Eris first. In Discovering Eris: The Symbolism and Significance of a New Planetary Archetype, Le Grice assigns Eris to Libra.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Thank you for the suggestion! However, the mythological Eris, as well as the Eris which seems active both in my chart and in the cultural shift which got underway from the 1970s on does not seem particularly attuned with justice...

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
The symmetry with the other planets would point to a Venusian sign for Eris:
- Uranus "dethroned" Saturn's dual rulership by taking over Aquarius
- Neptune "dethroned" Jupiter's dual rulership by taking over Pisces
- Pluto "dethroned" Mars's dual rulership by taking over Scorpio (Mars will regain Scorpio as Pluto's influence wanes).

Next in the Chaldean order is the Sun, but that already only rules Leo so we skip to Venus. Meaning it's either Taurus or Libra.
- Libra is the most archetypically "feminine" sign, as a counterpoint to the masculine Aries (which Mars kept), so it would seem to be better served as retained by Venus.
- Eris has been in Aries since well before its discovery and if it rules Libra, it must be in its detriment in Aries, and that doesn't seem to be the case. Eris works well in Aries. It may, in fact, be exalted in Aries.
- The symbolism of Libra doesn't fit anyway. Libra is characterized by urbane sophistication, civility, and manners; its shadow is indecision, wavering, and yielding to another's lead. This doesn't sound like Eris.
- It DOES sound like certain aspects of Taurus, though - think The Bull Seeing Red.

It will be hard to tell. By the time Eris actually enters Taurus in 2044, the Saturn return of its demotion will have long since passed and it will be as minor an influence as Ceres is today. (For the record, I think Ceres ruled Virgo during the period of its influence.)

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: Do you have a sense of why so many religious folks of diverse faiths seem to be obsessed with telling other people what to do?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Because that's hardwired into the structure of prophetic religions -- the kind of religion that was founded by someone specific, rather than growing up naturally out of the experience of a people. At the origin of every prophetic religion is somebody who was convinced that he (or, very rarely, she) knew the bona fide absolute truth, and everyone else was wrong like the wrongest of very wrong things. With that kind of role model at the center of a religion, believers in that religion are going to default to the same role and spend their time insisting that they've got the truth and everyone ought to listen to them. It strikes me as a waste of time, but then that's one of the reasons I don't follow a prophetic religion.

comments.content:
Hello JMG,

I'd be grateful if you could give me a hint on how I could proceed in my current situation.

I would describe my world view as "Christian animism". I believe in the presence of God, his angels, the saints and the souls of the departed - and of course their demonic couterparts.
Intellectually I also presume the intelligence of heavenly bodies, the presence of other gods, nature spirits, and those beings that one could call "faery".

I would very much like to build up good relations and connections to local spirits and my departed ancestors.

As I live in an European country in a region where my family has been in for centuries (family tree goes back reliably to the 15th century), I would like to do this in a way that is also integrated with my culture and the history of the area.

I read your books on Druidry and feel a certain pull towards it, but I'm not at all interested in changing my religion or starting a lifelong training in magic. I just want to be able to connect to the roots of my culture and interact in a respectful way with those unseen elements that have been with us for millenia but are nowadays neglected in our materialist society.

I already immersed myself in the local history, folklore, and legends with the intention of building up a shared inventory of symbols, so that there might develop some contact in dreams/synchronicities, which I plan to track in a notebook. So far without much success. I also spend time in places that I feel might be meaningful or likely to be someones(?) dwelling place (historic sites, undisturbed nature, hilltops, riverbanks) and try to tune into the mood of the place.

Next to the things mentioned above, I pray regularly and try to write down and analyze my dreams.

Is there some basic practice that I could start, that would help me in my efforts? Or should I better leave it alone, as to not get myself noticed by the wrong critter and end up eaten?

Thank you.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: If you're comfortable with your own religion, by all means keep with it! What I'd advise is that you look for traditions in your own region about what practices you can perform and what ways of interaction with spirits used to be common there. Intensive practice of Christian devotion can also help a great deal, especially if your version of Christianity is comfortable with saints and angels.

comments.title: Gods in myths
comments.content:
Might you (and readers who have experience with this) have a suggestion about how to read and understand/use myths as a first step toward having a relationship with gods when the myths emphasize very petty behaviors and less than appealing humanlike characteristics? I'm finding it hard to figure out where and who to approach - the Greek gods always appealed to me when I was young in spite of the sense of bemusement i felt then too that prevented me from taking the stories or beings seriously. But I figured I should start with the stories that drew me first.

Turns out I'm not so inspired to approach jealous, lustful, quick to anger, murderous, conniving, arrogant, self involved "personages" so I figure either I've not passed the flake filter, I've not made some sort of necessary conceptual leap, I'm looking at the wrong pantheon, or I need to find better sources for myths. Or something else altogether?

Any suggestions much appreciated!

replies.title: Re: Gods in myths
replies.content: People have been fretting about that since the Greeks hit their Age of Reason. There are various ways to make sense of it, the most traditional being to recognize the myths as symbolic in nature, not literally true. Still, if the myths really turn you off, you might want to consider a different pantheon.

replies.title: Re: Gods in myths
replies.content: OK, thanks. Looks like I'll need to keep reading just to make sure (that I've given it a fair shake and made room for symbolism), and then read other myths if needed.

replies.title: Myth Taken
replies.content:
What is your purpose or purposes in establishing a relationship with powerful beings? Do you have a favor to ask of Zeus/Jupiter? Perhaps a blessing on the expansion of your small business endeavours? Then why not approach him as you would a powerful, rich, businessman who can give you advice, connections, or a capital investment? If you butter him up, admire his tactics, and strenuously fail to mention your profound disapproval of his propensity for adultery you might profit from the relationship.

If, however, you want a safe and successful pregnancy and you appeal to Hera for her aid, it might be best not to have too cozy a connection with the Archetype of the philandering husband. Respect and delicate sympathy might go a long ways with her; she does not often figure in the myths as anything but spiteful and vindictive, but that is the fate of many a stay-at-home mom who nevertheless has much to offer when she is appropriately asked.

Power is power is power; but in polytheism, power is not universal. Power is decentralized and distributed. That which resides in the province of one Divinity is probably absent from the others. Why would negotiating with the gods and goddesses be different from relating to powerful or helpful humans?

Seeking Wisdom from Athena/Minerva? Might need to cut back on beer and wine and other stimulants dedicated to Dionysus. Also a show of respect for her Big Daddy might not hurt.

Do you wish to praise Aphrodite for showering you with so much proof of her power in this world, and worship her for her influence over the minds and hearts of many? Or beg her to release you from an addiction to pornography by increasing your chances for real instead of virtual sexual pleasures? Well, that might be tricky if you have also appealed to Artemis for success in the Hunt or fleetness of foot in sports. She might tell you to make up your mind about which body part you want to promote to first place.

If you should choose Kwan Yin or Our Lady for her compassionate enfoldment of human error in her mild, motherly arms, then you too will have your work cut out for you in this world.

If you desire to relate to One God who embodies all Divine Qualities, then you might have to develop more mercy, forgiveness, patience with folly, and a disturbingly cool, cosmic sense of humor to get along well.

If you choose the One Goddess to counterbalance the Greek pantheon’s clear bias toward patriarchy, then you may have to act as her agent in the world, speak up for her values more often than you like, risking ridicule from your friends and sore alienation from your family in service to her. Divine Service is not without costs and consequences.

If you are not inclined to worship, praise, seek favors, nor desirous to emulate excellent Qualties, then why concern yourself at all? If you are simply curious about the world inhabited by the Divine Beings then perhaps you could try to relate to Hermes in a detached intellectual way, keeping in mind that he has trickery up his chlamys, likes playing practical jokes and is a lot smarter than you are.

Egyptian, Asian, Native American and many other cultures have their own pantheons as well. Perhaps a deep meditation, daydream-style imaginative visioning technique (scrying, guided meditation) or session of self-examination will reveal your soul purpose for seeking and more clearly point you towards the Being(s) best for you to court a closer connection. But take care to approach with the same alert caution you would use when walking around in a coal-fired electrical power plant while carrying a steel toasting fork in one hand.

replies.content: Can you tell something about Apollo?

replies.title: Re: Myth Taken
replies.content:
Very well said, Anonymous.

In my mid-twenties, I chose the One Goddess, and for the next twenty-five years or so, my calling and my life were exactly as you describe.

My upbringing gave me a strong inclination toward monotheism, rather in the way that people who were brought up Christian import the world view and values of Christianity into their initial explorations of other religions.

My Goddess monotheism has gradually relaxed as I get more familiar with other ways of conceiving of and interacting with various beings. I'm sticking to the commitments I have made, of course, but believe I have some leeway in how I carry them out.

replies.title: Re: Gods in myths
replies.content:
Many contemporary polytheists write about their engagement with their pantheon's myths. Here's an example, interpreting the myth of Marsyas in the light of dedication to art: https://krasskova.wordpress.com/2017/03/26/apollo-and-marsyas-the-nature-of-art/

You definitely will want to read Sallustius, an ancient author who explains the several levels on which myths can be understood. Find out the meanings and etymologies behind the divine names; untranslated words hide many clues that would have been obvious to the ancients.

I'd advise learning discursive meditation from our good host's books and working through each myth bit by bit once you understand the technique. For example, I started out thinking about the domains and rulerships of each deity, then how they related to one another within a whole system (what are the relationships that join Love, Craft, and War? What links the horse to the sea, and wisdom with the olive tree? Why is the god of the underworld the god of wealth? and so on and so forth).

As soon as you can bring yourself to do so, build a shrine and start to pray and make offerings. There really is no substitute for living religion, and the only way to know the gods is to come into relationship with them. I'd say that the practice of prayer and offering is primary and myth auxilary, rather than the other way around.

It may be helpful to remember that the myths we know are only certain versions of a few myths; there's no telling how many other myths and how many other versions were lost. Most of the myths we still have were interpreted by poets and playwrights for their own purposes and audiences--it might be comparable to learning Christianity from a combination of "Good Omens," "The Life of Brian," and a couple trade reprints of the "Lucifer" comic book. They are also those which were allowed to survive by later monotheistic copyists, who very likely had other priorities than maintaining the dignity and divinity of the gods they were trying to replace. In other words, the lore as we possess it today is not Holy Writ, but keys to the Mysteries of the gods--Sister Crow

All the Gods are immeasurably wiser, better and mightier than a human. They have personalities, and myths can help illustrate that personality in ways that can be understood, but then again, if one looks at any of the major Gods of Ancient Greece, the list of titles is so immense that we see the immense capacity of a God. And I couldn't agree more that living religion -- which is to say a living *relationship -- is much more important than myth.

to my mind, myth is a good way of signal testing; if I'm praying to a specific God and hearing specific words and tone in my heart, it's god to understand the myth so that I can better make sure there I'm correctly understanding Who exactly is on the other end. To my sensibility, I'd much rather know someone who's amazing than know things about someone who is amazing.

replies.title: Re: Gods in myths
replies.content:
Hi Anonymus,

I’ve come to regard the Greek myths as the Kubuki theater of the Gods. They are not egotistical, petty, spiteful, controlling, blood-thirsty, or what-have you. Rather, they are playing roles through which they might instruct and help us. They really are beneficent, and really do care about us. The traits they embody reflect human traits, and are presented to us as a mirror. And I can recall myself in a devotional moment when I confessed to Hera, "...and I don't a believe a word Homer said about you...".

For example, in the Orphic Hymns, Mars [Ares] is clearly referred to in his multiple aspects. The opening line describing as "Magnanimous, unconquerable, boisterous Mars", which I take as his core nature, but the hymn goes on to describe a blood-lusting monster, then abruptly switches with an appeal for him to yield to Venus and Bacchus, and the hymn concludes almost with a benediction of peace. I interpret these verses as mirroring the dark side of the male psyche, and point in the direction one must proceed to transcend it.

Speaking of Homer, I would add a caveat: He seemed to have his own agenda, and so didn’t shy from depicting the Gods and Goddesses in a particularly unflattering light (A digression: FWIW, I suspect Homer was a would-be Western Confucius, but he had to be overly subtle and indirect to avoid getting his own throat cut, and the barbaric elites who listened rapturously to him didn’t pick up his intended message…).

Anyway, my 2 cents. I hope it helps.

comments.title: Nature of will
comments.content:
Hi JMG
Another question if I may.
Regarding the nature of will, you've said that will is a renewable resource but also talked about will power being locked up in conflicting spells. replies.title: Re: Nature of will
replies.content: Will is like any other renewable resource -- if you extract it at a rate faster than it's produced, you run out. (Think of forests or fish as examples -- cut too many trees or catch too many fish and you outrun the replenishment rate of the resource.) So yes, you can end up with so much of your will committed that you don't have any available for other uses. People do this all the time -- think of any genuine fanatic you've ever known, so caught up in one particular act of will that everything else goes by the boards.

replies.title: Re: Nature of will
replies.content:

If this the right interpretation then it would appear to be a good idea to put definite time frames or limiting conditions on any spells to prevent a build up of will power drains over time.

Thanks

replies.title: Re: Nature of will
replies.content: It depends to some extent on the kind of magic you practice -- natural magic doesn't involve much will, and neither does astrological magic, since they get their power from other sources. With regular ceremonial magic, though, yes, that's pretty much accurate.

replies.title: Re: Baptism as an adult?

comments.title: Natural Magic
comments.content:
We spent a good deal of time yesterday at some dear friends' house for my wife's birthday. The wife of the couple (both Pisceans) is one of the sweetest people we know, but it emerged over the course of the 5 hour conversation that her life is a real mess. She wakes up every morning feeling like an absolute failure (her words) and basically has to martial herself and soldier on to get through each day. She feels like her mother always judges her harshly, despite the fact that she married a doctor and is fairly well off, and that she always shows up at just the wrong time, and enlists her younger sister to gang up on her.

Towards the end of our visit she was showing us her collection of magical goods - books, herbs, candles, divination card decks, etc - that she stores in a trunk in her bedroom to keep unwelcome eyes out.

First thing our eyes were drawn to in their upstairs bedrooms was a handful of really egregious feng shui problems - mirrors across from the bed, plants in bedrooms, foo dogs on the dresser, etc - so we did some curing there immediately. (She was quite open to making the changes there on the spot.)

Earlier than that though, we had noticed quite a bit of opal jewelry on her, and I mentioned, when her husband was out of the room (out of fear that it was a gift from him - it was), that the opals might be responsible for a fair portion of the issues she was having. Again, the opal ring came right off and went on her dresser.

Long story longer, we are attempting to put together a package of recommendations for her in an attempt to get things back on track. Here's what we've got so far:

1) the opals need to stay off her body the vast majority of the time, and in a magically-insulating bag made of silk or linen, correct?

2) to reverse the bad luck of the misty opals (especially for a March Pisces) I think she should wear a fiery stone with grounding earthy qualities, say, carnelian (Sun in Virgo) or gifted diamonds (Mars in Taurus). Does that make sense?

3) she likes herbal baths, and in fact I think that's one of the primary expressions of her magic. In my research last night I came up with a bath of dittany of Crete, High John the Conqueror, and lovage. Again, mostly solar and earthy astrology, with a focus on driving out venemous hostility, getting the better of her oppressors, and improving her romantic life (because let's face it, improved bedroom relations are good for many ills:) Any additions, subtractions, or corrections?

4) their boys (9 and 5) sleep directly through the Jack and Jill bathroom from them, in the feng shui "coffin" position, feet pointed at the open bathroom door, which is terribly inauspicious. So we advised closing the bathroom door for the first night, reorienting the beds ASAP, and we're working up pa kua numbers for all 4 of them and offering recommendations for rearranging all the beds. I recall very clearly how that helped me immediately, before even sleeping in the new position! Which was one of my 1st TSW moments.

At your recent recommendation as well as the advice of my druid animal oracle last week, I will be buying your Earth Divination, Earth Magic book soon and learning geomancy, but for the moment feng shui is the design toolkit we have, so that's what we've used here. Any additional thoughts?

Apologies for the length, and thanks so much for your time, good sir. Our monthly appreciation will be on its way directly...😁
Tripp

replies.title: Re: Natural Magic
replies.content: All of that sounds very sensible. I wonder if a well-chosen affirmation might help her, too; I'll be doing a post here fairly soon on how to craft affirmations, so that might be worth sending her way.

replies.title: Re: Natural Magic
replies.content: I am eagerly awaiting said affirmation-crafting post here!

replies.title: Re: Natural Magic
replies.content:
Wonderful! Thank you again. And I think that's probably a great idea. At the moment she has a string of cut out letters on the wall on her side of the bed made for her by a new agey friend that reads "YOU ARE F-ING AWESOME".

What's your take on that from an affirmation perspective?
Tripp

replies.title: Re: Natural Magic
replies.content:
Not good. First of all, it's ambiguous, given the extremely polymorphous nature of the profanity in question; her mind might well take it to mean "you are having sex with Awesome," which would doubtless strengthen her emotional connection with her husband but other than that wouldn't do much.

The broader problem with it, and with other affirmations that aim solely at boosting self-esteem, is that it feeds the habit of vanity without actually changing anything, thus creating an internal conflict between the affirmation and the recognition that she, her actions, and her life are anything but awesome. That's a major source of the kind of angry and brittle egotism that's so pervasive in the modern world.

Finding a constructive affirmation for her situation would have to start by figuring out what beliefs and assumptions are helping to feed her problems, and choosing an affirmation that applies to those. Her feelings of being a failure, and her continuing emotional dependence on her mother's withheld affection, are good places to start. Those are best tackled indirectly, of course -- for feelings of failure, for example, she might try something like this: (breathing in) "I am one with the One Life of the Universe." (breathing out) "It flows through me and gives me strength." (Never tackle charged emotional issues directly; that rarely works. A good initial affirmation undercuts the presuppositions behind the emotional issues, and so makes the issues lose their charge without ever having resistance to support themselves against.)

replies.content:
Many thanks for the sneak peak of your upcoming post on affirmation-crafting! Much obliged. 😁
Tripp

comments.title: Regular banishing effects on talismans
comments.content:
Would doing the LBRP and BRH while wearing a charged talisman (elemental or planetary) diminish the power of the talisman?

Would regular performance of the rituals demagnetise other magical objects (from different traditions) that are in the home?

Thanks!

replies.title: Re: Regular banishing effects on talismans
replies.content:
1) Yes. Wrap the talisman in silk or linen while you're doing banishing rituals nearby.

2) It really depends on the nature of the objects and the manner in which they've been consecrated. As a general rule, though, don't banish in the same room as anything you want to keep consecrated, unless you've got it covered in silk or linen, or inside a box made of metal.

replies.title: Re: Regular banishing effects on talismans
replies.content:
Dear JMG,

In the future, please include this piece of advice prominently at the beginning of instructions for any banishing ritual.

I have been doing the SOP according to the instructions in this blog from Day One. I usually do it in the room where my altar and most of my magical tools and supplies are kept. That room is ideally suited to performing the SOP, because it is nearly circular and has clerestory windows oriented exactly to the cardinal directions.

I would hate to think I was demagnetizing my tools by my daily practice. Fortunately, I incline to natural magic and kitchen witchery more than ceremonial magic. I own some things that have been blessed, and supplies that were harvested or compounded according to planetary hours, but the primary consecrated ritual object I have in the room (as best I recall) is stored in a silk pouch in a drawer, so it should be all right.

I also have a couple of consecrated wands that are wrapped in cotton inside another wooden drawer. They have a good deal of magical and sentimental value for me. I have just wrapped the bundle of the more potent of the two in a big silk scarf. I hope the ritual markings on them and their history of use have given them some resistance to being banished.

replies.title: Re: Regular banishing effects on talismans
replies.content: The SoP is not the LBRP! You shouldn't have any negative effects at all from it -- if you'll check the wording of the SoP, you'll find that its banishings are much more narrowly focused. The LBRP banishes more generally, thus the nature of my reply to the OP.

replies.title: Re: Regular banishing effects on talismans
replies.content: I'm guessing #2 doesn't apply to ritual tools. If so, is it because the tools are meant to channel energies as needed, and the talisman to be a reservoir of specific energies?

replies.title: Re: Regular banishing effects on talismans
replies.content: Exactly. A working tool doesn't store energies, it channels and directs them, and regular banishings keep them good and clean.

replies.title: Re: Regular banishing effects on talismans
replies.content: If banishing weakens talismans then how do working tools like wands get by unscathed?

replies.title: Re: Regular banishing effects on talismans
replies.content: Because a working tool isn't a talisman, of course. See my comment immediately above.

comments.title: Magical Reasons on Parenthood and Children
comments.content:
Someone asked last week: Are there any magical reasons that can influence whether or not someone wants to have children?

And you replied: Sure. What kind of thing are you thinking of, though? "Magical reasons" cover a whale of a lot of territory.

Hopefully the OP will jump back in this week, because I'm very interested in this topic and just want to shout from the rooftops "Tell me everything!"

1. One question I can think of amid this tornado is in regards to karmic ties. You've said before that people often forego raising children their last several human lifetimes in order to reduce karmic ties (correct me if I didn't remember that right). Does this vary between pregnancy-children verses adopted children?

2. The most obvious reason I can think of in terms of magical reasons telling a person to *not* have children would be time. Skipping parenthood frees up a lot of time, even if one still has to work for a living. That time could be put toward turning inward.

Hopefully others will chime in as well; I'm very fascinated by this. Thank you so much.

replies.title: Re: Magical Reasons on Parenthood and Children
replies.content: Those are certainly issues to keep in mind, as they would be any time you're considering something as vitally important as parenthood. When you have a child you're committing yourself to put the child's interests ahead of your own for the next 18 years, and if for whatever reason this isn't something you can do, parenthood isn't a good idea. There are also karmic issues -- and those affect any close relationship, including adoptive parenthood.

replies.title: Re: Magical Reasons on Parenthood and Children
replies.content:
If we’re going by Dion Fortune’s definition of magic as the art of change of consciousness in accordance with one’s will, and these days I certainly am, then yes, every ounce of my decision not to have children was and is a magical act.

I always knew I didn’t want children, though when I was a child myself I had a dream of my older self at a piano surrounded by happy, delighted children, leading them in song. This ended up being rather prophetic. For me, this did not seem like the appropriate era to have kids. Never did — I always felt it from the core of my being since age 4, when I was given baby doll toys to play with and had a hard time cultivating interest in them. My teenage years in the 1980s were marked by paranoia about pregnancy and AIDS, the legacy of which has been a disconnect from sexuality in general as it was always linked with my worst fear, reproduction. In my early 30s, when I had been married about five years, I had myself sterilized during a gynecological surgery for another issue. This was a major victory for me. Young women feel a tremendous pressure to procreate, and sterilization is difficult to attain. Your sanity is always tacitly questioned even though it would seem far more crazy to have children before one is fully prepared to do so.

The primary reasons I did not have children and the changes of consciousness that resulted from my decision are:

1. I wanted what was right for me; having a child wasn’t it. Result: I believe I’m much happier because I acted in accordance to my own desires rather than society’s desires. I’ve become a quiet role model for young people who can now see an example of someone who stood up to pressure to procreate and did not let it defeat her.

2. I wanted more time. Result: I’ve had time to start my own successful business that I believe brings genuine music education to my community, write a few novels, produce an album of songs, spend quality time with my own parents and husband, and dote on my cat and hamsters. My life feels serene and fulfilled most of the time. Though I’m sure I would have loved my child/children, I knew I didn’t have time to give them the time they deserved.

3. I wanted to err on the side of caution. Raising children isn’t a job one can do half-#$$ed. Sooooo much can go wrong. Result: Now I just worry twice as much about everything else that can go wrong!! Problem solved LOL

replies.title: Re: Magical Reasons on Parenthood and Children
replies.content: Glad you posted this. My wife does not want children, and I mostly do not (in my early 40s, male) but sometimes I get this strange, strong impulse to have children, but I think that part of that is psychological. I see other people with children, playing happily outside or whatever, and this also triggers the urge, and maybe the sense that time is running out. Yet I want my time, there are things, like magic, I want to do and committing to children has never felt entirely right. But that urge, when it comes up, is surprisingly strong...

comments.title: Timing of Practices
comments.content:
Last week someone was asking about splitting up their practice with divination and/or meditation in the mornings and ritual in the mornings. You said “It would be best to move the entire practice to the evening, so you can do it all together. It's not really a good idea to break things up.”

I have two questions about this:

1. I am usually in my room for ritual/meditation around 6:30am so that I can leave for work by 7:30, this usually works fine, but on the rare occasion that I oversleep in the morning (looking back over my journal it appears this has happened just under 30 times in the last year, usually clustered right around the new moon, so it’s not terribly common), how do you recommend approaching this when this does happen? In that situation is it better to A: do my ritual, go to work, then meditate when I get home, B: do my whole practice after work that day even though it’s out of my usual cycle or C: do what I can in the morning then come back around and do a full practice later in the evening? I’ve tried both A and B, and in my experience B leaves me feeling off all day without any morning practice, but A tends to lead to really disconnected and uncomfortable meditation sessions. What’s been most effective in those situations it seems has been a banishing ritual followed by a minute or two of four-fold breathing in the morning and then a full practice in the evening after I get home. That presses the one ritual session a day limit but it’s been the only approach to that event that doesn’t leave me feeling off in some way. (Once more, this is for emergency situations where I only have 15-20 minutes in the morning rather than my usual hour, not for general practice.)

2. I usually do divination last thing before I go to bed, this lets me review my previous day’s divination in the light of the day’s events, wrap up my journal for the day, then start the next day’s on the adjacent page with the next day’s seed thought and the next day’s meditation along with a brief description of what I think it means, and it allows me to internalize the divination theme while I sleep since patterns in that practice filter into my subconscious during my daily review which helps me to keep it in my background awareness the next day. It also allows me to consolidate my divination practice since I am supposed to examine the previous day’s reading at the end of the day while the day is still fresh. It makes sense to lead that right into my next divination. comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
On a previous MM some time ago, you talked about the concept that you can't reach a more perfect manifestation of a deity than your own consciousness will permit. I can't quite wrap my head around the specifics of this idea, so I was hoping you could comment on a few points.

1. If one's manifestation of a deity will always correspond with one's moral/spiritual condition, wouldn't this result in a deity that only ever tells you what you want to hear, and could never push you to a higher/lower standard than the ones you're already striving for? I would assume not, as this seems to contradict a lot of existing religious experience, but if the deity is always a reflection of the worshiper in some regard, I'm not certain how this would work.

2. If, to use your example, if the Westboro Baptists are contacting a demonic distortion of Jesus, is there a daimonic version of Jesus on one of these planes that has no distortion at all? Or do you have to go all the way to the noetic level to find the "true" manifestation of a given deity?

3. Say you wanted to invoke Thor as the patron god of flowers and kittens. This seems like it would be less a distortion of Thor and more of a total category mistake. Do you think the actual Thor would respond? Is it possible that another deity who did have those associations would respond instead, claiming to be this version of Thor? I'd be interested to hear your take on how, if the gods are filtered through our own spiritual condition, one's intentions/expectations (however misguided) might come into play when working with them.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: 3. My guess is Thor would whack you over the head, tell you "I AM NOT A GOD OF FLOWERS AND KITTENS!" and then direct you to a god who is.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) A deity will manifest itself to you in the best form you are capable of perceiving. Most people can perceive virtues they can't yet attain, so a deity can always encourage you to be your best and to strive to make your best a little better.

2) Human beings aren't capable of experiencing the true manifestation of a deity -- not without being blasted into nonexistence. (See the Greek myth of Semele for an example -- or for that matter Enoch, "who walked with God and was not.") We can get very slight glimpses of them on the noetic level, and we can interact with their manifestations on the daimonic/psychic level. All these latter manifestations are partial, but some are more balanced and benefic to humans than others -- and then there are demonic perversions of the divine images. The Westboro Baptist folks aren't worshiping Jesus at all, but a demon of hatred and self-righteous egotism.

3)Depends on the deity. Thor would probably whack you good and hard. Some other deities would ignore you. Some -- Odin comes to mind -- would glance at you with an ungentle smile and manipulate you for their own purposes, using your wilful ignorance as an instrument; whether you would survive the process is an interesting question. (Deities don't like being treated as vending machines, or told by humans how they should appear to the latter...)

replies.title: Deities as vending machines

I have a group I meet with on a regular basis, all refugees from a new thought church that imploded, destroyed by a toxic minister.

Page 28:”spirit of the living fire, pour thyself on me that I may be consumed in the flames of purification,,,”
This seems like a particularly dangerous request, to ask from the Lord of Karma (you mentioned some time ago something called a karma dumping run, and nobody ever tries it twice...)

replies.title: Re: Deities as vending machines
replies.content: Oh dear gods. The person who wrote that book had his head clenched firmly between his nether cheeks. (a) Angels are not your servants. They are beings of a far higher order than humanity; you can invite their presence and ask for their help, but that's it -- and if you're the kind of person who would try to treat angels that way, angels will be aware of that, and calmly and with perfect compassion will let you go bugger yourself. (b) Saturn as the angel of success and fulfullment -- that just has me shaking my head and hoping to be a long ways away from anyone who tries that. Saturn is the lord of strict justice; he gives you exactly what you deserve, not what you think you deserve, and he's perfectly willing to give it to you using a baseball bat across the teeth. (c) That fire invocation is seriously problematic unless you're braced for the consequences, yep. (d) Creeping on planetary intelligences is a really bad idea for practical reasons, as well as just generally sleazy. In general, no, you're not being too literal and overreacting. If the members of the group are lucky, they'll be too inconstant and incompetent to pursue the practices in the book to the point of getting clobbered. You should be fine attending discussions, though, and you can always try to offer good advice if someone's life crashes and burns as a result of doing that book's workings.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Okay, first of all, you're fudging the modes of perfection. In a fourth house question, you need a mode of perfection between the first and fourth houses, not between the first and some other house! comments.title: Monotheist omnipresent, omniscent, all-powerful God vs. polytheist "not so much"
comments.content:
Hi JMG. Coming from a monotheist background it has been an interesting journey for me reading your thoughts, and certainly the polytheist concepts and ideas learned here has been challenging for me. Aside from being more powerful and more knowledgeable about the universe than humans, it appears that the various spirits, gods, and entities that inhabit the universe are rather like humans in that they can be deceitful, truthful, weak, strong, insightful, ignorant, have good intentions, bad intentions, etc etc, just like us. At least that's the way it appears to me.

In the monotheist view it used to be comforting to reside in the belief that despite what happened in life an all-powerful, etc etc God was in control of it all and directed everything that happened and created the universe.

That not being the case in the polytheistic worldview (as I understand it), it still seems that all of the gods, spirits, entities are in some way subject to some kind of all encompassing force or power (non-personal?)that "runs" the universe.

My evangelical friends would like to know "ok so if a personal monotheistic God is not running the universe, who or what is?" Is it run by committee? So would it be somewhat on target to say that all the gods, spirits, etc are subject to some kind of overarching "power" that we might call the unmanifest, for lack of a better concept?

Although I think I remember you telling someone once that there was probably always some god more powerful than the others I suppose that doesn't mean that god would always be so, and another could at some point assume that role.

Could it be that in our corner of the universe that our solar system is ruled by the god that manifests as our sun, and therefore all the others in our solar system are subject to it? And our sun god and solar system subject to even greater powers that are unknowable on this plane?

Thank you for your efforts to help recovering monotheists

David (not by the lake)

replies.title: Re: Monotheist omnipresent, omniscent, all-powerful God vs. polytheist "not so much"
replies.content: In most polytheist traditions, nobody runs the universe, just as nobody created the universe. There is no Big Boss. The Unmanifest isn't a person, it's a condition of (non)being, so it doesn't run anything. In many occult teachings, the Solar Logos is the wisest and most powerful being in this solar system, but it's not omnipotent, and is busy with its own projects and may or may not be interested in what you're doing with your life, say. The universe, and the solar system for that matter, are not committees but communities -- you might call them ecosystems. Does that help make sense of it all?

replies.title: Re: Monotheist omnipresent, omniscent, all-powerful God vs. polytheist "not so much"
replies.content: Who is "in charge" of the food web?

comments.title: A small ars combinatoria system?
comments.content:
Do you have any advice for coming up with a small set of concept/questions for a pocket-sized combinatorial system?

I really like the list of concept/questions that you included in the appendix to the translation of On The Shadows of the Ideas, but I find that for a beginner, or maybe for daily use, it might be a large.

I was thinking I would like to develop skill in this sort of thinking system by having a small set of concepts, maybe 5 - 7, that could be used for thinking about, say, a newspaper article or something like that, maybe a 10 minute exercise altogether.

I also liked the idea of using the Tarot or the geomantic figures as a basis of the concepts. For a smaller system, I was wondering about using the four elements & the quintessence, or maybe the seven classical planets.

Another question about the larger sample Tarot-based combinatorial system: using it to think about a particular topic, would one try to contemplate every question that arose from the various combinations, or is it more scanning through the combinations until one comes across a perspective on the topic that one hasn't considered, and which seems fruitful?

Once the concept/questions were memorized & associated with the letters/tarot cards, I found it pretty easy to spin though the combinations, but a lot of the questions were seeming a little repetitive or not that relevant to the topic at hand. I could see, however, juxtaposing the concept/questions with the topic at hand in order, ticking through the dial, and stopping to contemplate whenever the combinations seem to fit.

As an aside, working with this system reminds me a lot of the thinking systems of Edward de Bono, which I experimented with as a teenager, especially this CoRT exercises and the Six Thinking Hats system. He seems a world away from renaissance esotericism, I think a lot of people got turned off his work as he tried to gear it to corporate workshops, etc. He did have his start though in the cybernetics field in the 1960s, I think his techniques could have a place in systems thinking type exercises.

replies.title: Re: A small ars combinatoria system?
replies.content:
I worked up one for the Dolmen Arch course based on some scraps of the original course. It has seven concepts and seven questions:

Concepts: Being, Becoming, Source, Substance, Power, Purpose, Value
Questions: Whether, What, How, Who, Where, When, Why

You ask each question of each concept: whether it is, what it is, how it is, who (or whose) it is, where it is, when it is, why it is; whether it becomes, what it becomes, and so on. It's easy to learn and use, and seems to work fairly well.

replies.title: Re: A small ars combinatoria system?
replies.content: I've found the Dolmen Arch system works well.

comments.title: two questions
comments.content:
Greetings, Your Archdruid Emeritus-ness,

Long time reader, first time commenter. I have been working with the system presented in your "Druidry Group" books (TDH/DMH) with good results so far; and I have two questions.

1. Each Ogham few has its associated tree, but some of the trees in the list aren't found in the Northeast U.S. (grapevine, blackthorn, and gorse, for example), and other trees which are very common here don't have an associated few (maple, for example). Is there some method of figuring out which native tree might go with which few beyond simply meditating on it and seeing what my intuition says?

2. You mention in the FAQ that your books fall into three groups, and no book from one group is compatible with books from another group. Can you explain why this is the case? The ritual practices in "The Celtic Golden Dawn" are different from TDH and DMH (and I can understand why one should probably stick with one set or the other) but the theory in the books doesn't seem incompatible to me. I'm thinking specifically of the different divination methods in each book. Can I use both the Ogham stick method from TDH/DMH and the "Druid Wand" method in CGD, and compare the results? Heck, could I compare both of those results with Tarot readings? I feel like each method kind of speaks a different language.

I look forward to hearing your thoughts on this. Thanks for being a great teacher!

replies.title: Re: two questions
replies.content:
1) I don't know of a way except for meditation and intuition.

2) The theories are similar, sure, but the practices are different, and the divination methods have been chosen so that their symbolism has other uses within the system. That's why I encourage people not to mix books from different systems of mine.

replies.title: Re: two questions
replies.content: I have approached it by looking up the ecological function (niche) of the tree of that few and then finding a similar tree in the same family with a similar ecological function that is native to or naturalized in my area (the US lower Midwest). For instance, I use serviceberry, Amelanchier arborea, rather than rowan for Luis, as they are both small deciduous trees in the rose family with berries that are favored by wildlife and also eaten by humans. This works for most of the trees, but there are two, gorse and heather, that don't have any member of the same family with that ecological function in my area. Sometime later on, when I return to meditating on the fews, I'll look again at the trees and see what 6 more years of practice brings to bear on the issue.

comments.title: Sacred Geography

No question this week but I do have a comment. As a gift I received a copy of The Discovery of Middle Earth: Mapping the Lost World of the Celts, by Graham Robb. Regular readers here who are into Druidry might be interested in this one, and I don't believe I have seen it mentioned here before.

The author has discovered, fairly convincingly in my opinion, a method by which the ancient druids mapped out Western Europe and placed important sites using their knowledge of mathematics and astronomy. Samurai_47

If they’re very young try some of that Kitten Milk Replacement you get from the vet, if you’re willing to put money into the project.  (I’ve never bought KMR, but the cat-rescue lady tells me it’s pricey.). Also, don’t give up; the hungrier they get, the more likely they are to enter the trap.  Good luck!

I don't know what your situation is, or what the place is like, or what the weather's like, but I have had some experience in things like this. The problem with my approach is that it takes time and patience.

I would (if I could) make myself comfortable somewhere where the kittens could see me, maybe with a good book and/or a cup of tea or coffee, and just relax. Kittens are curious as could be. I don't know if you are able to see them much, but when you do, just calmly and quietly say something like 'hello little one', and go back to your book. Bring a bit of that food, maybe place it somewhere 10 feet off or so to start. It's really elementary trust building, and if they're hungry enough, that should bring them in. You have to judge when it's time to try to maybe touch one, if they get close enough. Sometimes they just give in all at once, and they're now your kittens, for better of for worse :-) Sometimes it might take a couple-three sessions of this.

That's the best I can suggest - it can take a bit of time this way, but it has always worked for me in the past, and with various critters - from kittens to cats to dogs all the way up to horses. The minute they think you're 'trying' to 'catch' them, you're back to square one, so patience!

Fairly recently there was a half-feral barn cat that had gotten caught in a leg-hold trap, and was dragging the trap with its chain around my place - amazing how fast it could move! I employed this method, and had him in my lap in half and hour. How he cried! But we fixed him up.
